title: OpenAI's Identity Crisis, Datacenter Wars, Market Up on Iran News, Mamdani's First Tax, Swalwell Out

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right everybody, welcome back to the number one podcast in the world. We've got the core four here and dare I say the king of atoms, the king of atoms, yes. Captain Travis Kalanick is here. How are you doing brother?

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I'm pretty good, pretty good. I'm sitting here doing the podcast just next door to David.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Ha, yes. There you go.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Don't reveal our locations.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Please don't dox.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I didn't put my address out there, dude.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: No, that's true.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Don't worry, Mondummy did.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: He's outside your house right now, asking them to foreclose on it.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I'm trying to collect 3.9% or something.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, he decided there's no rich people left in New York, so he's looking for other cities to tax.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So is it, is it 3.9% a year? Is it per year or is it one time?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I don't know if the percentage has been released yet, but the speculation I've seen is 3.9%, but I don't think that's final.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: But yeah, it's a pied-a-terre tax, so if you have a second home, yeah, every year.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And by the way, it's for any home over 5 million. There's no homes under 5 million in Manhattan. This is not a rich person tax. This is within 15 miles of Midtown Manhattan, you're paying an extra tax. I don't know.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: But only, but J-Cal, only if it's a pied-a-terre. So what it means is that the most elastic part of the market is what they're targeting for this tax.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, it means second homes.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Well, you have a—the most elastic part of the market is what they're targeting for this tax. So in other words, people who don't live in New York, who just have it as a second or third home, who could buy that property anyway, are now being taxed the most. So what do you think that's going to do? It's going to have a massive impact on demand for second homes in New York, which will crash the whole market.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. Congratulations Mondummy.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: But in a weird way, that'll be good for housing affordability in New York.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Well that, that's sort of the claim, but I don't think it'll be good for it because there'll be no incentive to build more.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All units matter. Every time you add units, people upgrade. And it's not like these are going to be low income housing. Like a penthouse on 57th Street or, you know, in Gramercy, that's not low income housing. You'd have to break it into seven units. Makes no sense. But by the way, I don't know if you guys saw the video, not to get too serious, but he's doxxing a certain billionaire who owns a certain place, and he's literally pointing at his home.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: No, I said that to you, Jason. He's not doxxing because everybody's known for years that Ken Griffin bought that place. Everybody knows that address. Everybody knows that unit. We all knew it. It was marketed widely. I don't think that's really doxxing. He doesn't live there, and everybody knew he owned it. It would be very different if it was a place where somebody was keeping their primary residence and you didn't know and they stood in front of the house. That I would agree with you. I think this one is a little bit more tenuous.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, fair enough. But what I will tell you is it's a dog whistle. Crazy people—and this thing's been seen by 30, 40, 50 million people now—it's a dog whistle to say, 'That's the next UnitedHealthcare CEO,' and in the week that a fire, a Molotov cocktail and a bullet get shot into Sam Altman's house, it's deadly serious. And if you—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: First, I always reverse it. What if a Republican sat outside of Bernie Sanders' second or third home and said, we should—this is his pied-à-terre, this is his summer home, we should add, you know, taxes to it, and I'm sure you could look up Bernie Sanders' home pretty easily. So just before you point at people's homes and say this is the villain, be careful folks, because then if something does happen to that person, like what happened to Sam Altman this week—you feel however you want about him, but nobody deserves to have their house firebombed or shot at, period, full stop.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I just think that it's going to kill the demand for, you know, maybe people who already have a home in New York like Ken Griffin, they probably are just going to suck it up and pay the tax and keep whatever they have. But if you were a person who was thinking about buying a pied-à-terre in New York, there's no way you would do it now.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Because you don't know what the tax rate's going to be, and it's going to keep going up.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: After a decade with interest and inflation, you've effectively almost doubled the price of your units. So if you're going to buy a $10 million unit, you're probably then looking at a $20 million purchase price just to break even after about 10 or 11 years. That's crazy. The math doesn't work anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And what is the downstream effect of these individuals not coming to New York, going to a Knicks game, going to a restaurant?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Nothing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Nothing? They spend money in New York. They have their birthday party in New York. They do all kinds of things.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I'm not a big fan of the pied-à-terre culture. I own one house. I don't like this whole multiple houses you're flitter-fluttering everywhere. It ruined London. Because when you look at London—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Chamath, where do you vacation?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I go to a hotel.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So my point is this, the way that it works is it's not just pied-à-terre. If you—if you own a home that you fully rent out, but that is not the primary residence of the person who is renting it, it's also a problem. So this is any, even like a two-week rental or one-week rental or a 30-day rental or even a five-year rental and it was somebody else's second home, the owner still gets hit. So the rental, the sort of rentals of like folks who want to come to that city or who don't want to own in that city, but it's a second place. The second place thing goes away. So maybe this is just good for hotels.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: I think it's good for hotels. But if you look at London, it's probably the best example where there was a lot of people who used London as a place to store assets. Real estate became the primary way in which they would do that. It hollowed out parts of London where it's not as if it was unlivable, it's just that it was unlived. Nobody was there. You'd drive around Chelsea, you'd drive around certain parts of London and it was like a ghost town on like a Thursday night or a Friday night.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That is an issue, with the land banking. It's like your Bitcoin is hollowing out a neighborhood.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Good, and and there's something there, but you know what the whole housing thing is complete cap and utter bullshit because if you move to a place like Austin or you are in Nevada or Florida, you see what happens when you allow people to build units. In Austin, three years in a row, rents and housing prices have gone down while net migration has gone up.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But that's been good, right, for Austin, you guys would say? Net good?

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Incredible. Amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Austin has like roughly doubled as a city over the past decade and yet the rent for, you know, whatever a one or two bedroom apartment's gone down.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Down? That's incredible.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: In other words, if you let people build to satisfy the demand you won't have this problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: And then who's stopping the building? It's Democratic cities, it's NIMBY people, and then in a Republican town, they're actually building units for affordability. So you have one group saying they care about affordability and they're doing nothing about it and they're stopping it. And in another place, they're like, we're just going to let you build because it's your right to build because it's your land. That's the approach in Texas. It's your land, you have the right to develop it, go.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: By the way, the high end of the market in London has basically turned over and collapsed. Sacks, to your point, like they introduced the stamp tax, which I think is equivalent to this tax that the New York City mayor is proposing. And if you look at London as a guide, the real estate market just bit it at the high end.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I don't think that's going to be good for that city or even the UK as a whole. There's a handful of cities where people do what you say, which is kind of park money there.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: The reason why they do that is because they believe that that city A has the rule of law and B is a unique world-class city that's going to keep appreciating.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: And if you have new management that doesn't really believe in the rule of law that basically keeps imposing all these arbitrary taxes, that money is going to flee and find other kinds of investments. People aren't going to park their money there. That has to be a bad thing for the city. It's like, you know, who cares if the top floor of that building where Ken Griffin lives is owned by one guy who isn't there that much?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: It's not going to affect the city that much, but having all these billionaires from all over the world decide to park money in that American city has to be good for the US.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Just like it's good for the UK. And if you give that up, then again, the money will just go somewhere else.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, to your point, the other thing is they're already paying taxes on the property. And because they're not there very often, they're not using city services.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So they're paying taxes on the property, they're not using city services.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They're profitable to the city. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Think about a developer who's underwriting some new project, you know, the fact that a whale like Ken Griffin is going to overpay in the sense of price per foot for that top floor might make that whole project pencil and this is what contributes to the vitality of New York is there's constantly development going on, there's constantly cranes and so you take out that part of the market that in effect...

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: that was price insensitive and was subsidizing all these projects and I think development's going to dry up to a large degree.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: He's paying, he must be paying three or four million in taxes every year and getting no services to your point Travis, good point like all profit for the city.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: London also did something else which is that they essentially crippled what's called non-dom status, which is the big tax orb if you moving or parking assets in London. And to your point Sacks, what did all the rich people do? They just redirected themselves to Zurich, to Lugano, to Milan and they they took advantage of more hospitable tax policy in other places. Now, what the people in Britain would tell you, I actually met with the UK government yesterday. They don't see a meaningfully enough measurable impact yet where they think it's a five alarm fire. So the real question is is it more of a slow bleed and a slow melt and at some point it's just hollowed out and it's very hard to reverse because there's not going to be a cataclysmic acute moment where people say oh my god we need to reverse these policies. It doesn't seem like that's in the offing.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: You know, LA did something a little different but similar is they introduced that 5% mansion tax that's on like all the areas except for like Beverly Hills and...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: San Francisco has that too.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah, San Francisco got that too. I think it's actually like 6%. In San Francisco, it's 6% over 25 million and it starts at 5 million. So there's a transfer tax, an excess transfer tax above $5 million property that scales up above 25 million to an extra 6% on top of your brokerage commission. So when you sell a house in San Francisco, you're paying 13% now.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Which is why you look at the transaction volume in the LA real estate market and it's just completely dried up. You know, people aren't doing the house flipping anymore, like that kind of stuff, because the transaction costs are too high. But it just shows, I mean again, this tax was imposed retroactively. For example, I had my house in SF and then they just take, you know, a couple of rooms of it, and we talked about this on the show in an app. But my my point is just your property is not safe in blue states and wealthy people who have a choice of where to park their money are going to increasingly realize that and they're not going to buy I think real estate in blue states is dangerous because the political class thinks that they can take a chunk of it. And you know, wealthy people are going to react to that and they're going to move their money elsewhere.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, let's go to topic number one, OpenAI is apparently suffering from a bit of an identity crisis on Sunday OpenAI's chief revenue officer Denise Dresser sent a four-page memo to employees, obviously it leaked immediately, probably the point of it. And she called out Anthropic. She said their 30 billion dollar run rate is cap, inflated by eight billion due to a revenue share and some accounting with AI model providers, Chamath you pointed that out in the last couple of weeks. Also, she said Anthropic's story is built on quote, fear restriction and the idea that a small group of elites should control...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: AI, obviously she's a fan of the pod. She also laid out OpenAI's pivoting and she said they are going hard after business customers and they want to win the agent platform layer. If you remember they hired the architect of the open-source project OpenClaude, they didn't acquire OpenClaude, so Peter Steinberger is working at OpenAI. Cynical people said hey maybe they want his next set of innovations to go inside of OpenAI's products as opposed to the open-source one. I kind of agree with that directionally. Obviously Perplexity computer is doing really well, they quadrupled their revenue. And I was over at xAI earlier this week with Elon, I can tell you he's got some very cool stuff coming. And this new model Spud is coming from OpenAI. Here's your Polymarket 75% chance Spud is released next week.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Additionally and we'll go to the panel in just a second. On Tuesday, two days after this memo came out, obviously people write these memos to go directly to the press, so they're obviously trying to undercut Anthropic's valuation and they feel that's the threat. That's my take. The FT cited anonymous OpenAI investors who are frustrated with the company's lack of focus. Here's the anonymous quote, 'Quote, you have ChatGPT a 1 billion user business growing 50 to 100 percent a year. What are you doing talking about enterprise and code? It's a deeply unfocused company.' And we talked about this, ChatGPT's market share is going down as the number of users is going up because Gemini and Claude gaining significantly and Meta just last week released their first proprietary model and Apple doesn't have a product in market yet but they do have a lot of users. So here's your Gen AI website traffic. Let's start Chamath with you, your thoughts on OpenAI. Should they be pivoting straight into business, developers, and getting focused on that or should they stay focused on the consumer where they are the verb?

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: As it relates to complex long horizon coding tasks, what I can tell you from my team at 8090 is Codex is better generally than Anthropic. And so what happens is for the more day-to-day work, I think it's more reliable to use the Claude ensemble of models, but when you're dealing with something that's very tricky and very complicated and a little bit more long horizon, Codex is really functional and really good. So I think if you're looking at it through the lens of OpenAI, what they're probably saying is hey hold on a second, if we allocate resources and just double down and crush consumer, that's probably three or four trillion of enterprise value, and then if we slowly refocus the company with the rest of the resources and double down on Codex and do something meaningful in enterprise, we can probably capture

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: two or three trillion there and now all of a sudden you can paint a picture for a seven, eight, nine trillion dollar market cap in the fullness of time, not tomorrow obviously.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: But Codex is really good and there's a business to be had in both. You have to separate the two businesses. You can't have a lot of overlap because there's too much context switching. You gotta let the consumer team run and then you gotta isolate the enterprise team and let them do what they think is right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Travis, there was a big debate going on amongst the investors. The FT piece also questioned the $850 billion valuation of OpenAI. Secondary markets have now priced Anthropic higher than OpenAI for the first time. This is the flipping in that people predicted. One investor said OpenAI would need to IPO at a valuation of $1.2 trillion for the last round to make any sense, but there's no buyers currently at the $850 billion valuation that OpenAI just closed according to Bloomberg. Travis, how do you handicap this race between these two, meaning frontier models?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Growth is king right now in this, in this world, in this segment. Growth is the whole damn thing. And if Anthropic is growing faster than OpenAI by a significant clip, the investors right now are going to play it forward. And they, you know, you start to get network effects around compute, network effects around the number of tokens you're pushing out for various customers, enterprise or consumer. And ultimately, you know, it's not great today, but how that plays into reinforcement learning and the things getting smarter over time, there's so many, there's so much upside to volume and scale that if they are growing faster at the same size, even if OpenAI's still growing, but if they are half the growth rate, a third the growth rate, a fifth the growth rate, I'd be worried.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And you saw this at Uber specifically when you accelerated away from the competitors like Lyft and DoorDash, yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you had to. Network effects was the whole thing at the end of the day. And network effects was based on scale. So yes, I'm sort of like coming from my very specific experience. But if you believe there's network effects with the scale of data that you have and the scale of customers and the revenue that is cash that's coming in that you redeploy into compute, and so say there's network effects of large compute...

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I'd be very worried if I'm OpenAI and seeing somebody growing faster at the same size.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So Friedberg, when you look at this race between these two giants, maybe your thoughts on the flywheel as it relates, as Travis is pointing out, to advantages in compute, reinforcement learning, and then also the ability to fundraise. One of the great things that Travis and the team did was as they were pulling away, they just sucked all the oxygen out of the room.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: using capital as a weapon. So your thoughts, Dave, on this high-stakes game. Because there's also a point at which, and I'll just end on this, there's a point at which you could run off the cliff. You raise so much money and you deploy it so fast and the revenue doesn't catch up to it, and then you go public and the markets don't believe the story. So your thoughts, Friedberg.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I don't know the financials of the two companies well enough. Obviously, Sam has no problem raising money. The guy, didn't he just close like a $150 billion round or something?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 122 billion.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Largest round ever raised in any market, I think, private or public.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, probably.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I mean, that's crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Even public, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So that doesn't seem to be an issue. What I've noticed is just the pace of innovation at Anthropic is, from my experience, unprecedented. I mean, their release cadence is extraordinary. They've basically supplanted OpenAI already with this release they did a few days ago, and then today the new Opus model got dropped. So there's something about the momentum, not necessarily just in user growth, but in how they're operating this business, that just seems to be head and shoulders above everyone else in the cadence of upgrades. If I look back six months ago, I think we were pretty heavy on Cursor and Gemini, and now I think we're probably 90% Anthropic in just the last six months in my organization. There's something very powerful about the flywheel they have going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but here's where I'd go, just real quick, Dave, is mad respect on a 120 or 130 billion dollar raise. I mean, this is obviously next level. But you can use capital and investment to acquire scale and network effects associated with it. But if somebody is getting that scale with revenue and let's call it contribution margin, contribution profit, efficiency will outstrip subsidy. And you can't just keep raising hundred billion dollar things forever. That's where the training wheels stop. And if Anthropic is funding theirs through revenue and other folks are funding it through investment, there's a short-term solve, but the long run is whoever is scaling their actual usage and system and ultimately with contribution profit that then soaks up the need for investment, that's a very scary machine if you're competing against it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Which is exactly, Sacks, what the legacy Mag 7 are doing. You have massive profits from Meta's core business, Google's core business, that are being redeployed into infrastructure. And even Tesla, which has a lot of profits, and SpaceX, which has a lot of cash on hand, and they're building out Colossus and other assets. Including Elon is working on building a fab as folks have been talking about. So Sacks, is there a chance for...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The legacy companies, the Mag 7s to compete in this, or are we looking at OpenAI and Anthropic are one and two and then everybody else can fight for third place and the bronze as it were?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Well, I think Google's clearly in the mix. I mean, DeepMind has an outstanding team, and I think Elon's still in the mix with xAI, and then you've got Meta also has the resources. They seem to be further behind, but they're going to compete. Look, let me just go back to the central premise here.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I agree that there's some valid criticism that OpenAI has been unfocused and should be, you know, moving forward, more focused in what they do. I have no idea, for example, what they're doing buying a podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That's not us. So I don't know what that was about.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If you were going to buy a tech podcast for a few hundred million dollars, I mean, we were here. We're here. We're here for you, Dario. Reach out.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Small potatoes for you guys though, small potatoes. They can't afford you. They can't afford you.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Yeah, they thought we were too expensive. Little did they know, we would have sold out.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Punch the ticket, Dario!

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: But look, this other part of the criticism of OpenAI, that they shouldn't do enterprise, is totally misguided. One of the reasons why they should have been more focused is to do more enterprise and get enterprise more correct. Now, why do I say that?

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: To Travis's point about growth rates, it's true that OpenAI and Anthropic, as of the beginning of Q2, so let's say two weeks ago, they were both around 30 billion of revenue. And that memo from that OpenAI employee was right that if you compare them on an apples-to-apples basis, that Anthropic is about 20% less because they are including revenue made by their channel partners. But that doesn't matter. What matters is the growth rate, again, to Travis's point.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Let me just put some numbers around this. The OpenAI growth rate's been around 3 to 4x a year. The Anthropic growth rate has been around 10x a year. So they went from, let's call it, 1 to 10 billion of ARR last year, and by the end of Q1 this year, they were already at 30 billion of, again, let's call it their revenue. And they're on their way, you know, like Brad Gerstner was saying on our podcast, I think in the last couple weeks, they're going to end this year at 80 to 100 billion, at least on the current trajectory.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: And so you can plot their revenue on a logarithmic graph. I mean, again, no one's ever seen anything like it before, where every unit on the y-axis is 10x and it's a straight line.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: It's crazy, right? So if it's taking Anthropic let's say one year to 10x and it's taking OpenAI two years to achieve a 10x, then it's obvious which one's going to win. Now, what is the reason for this? It's because Anthropic was very focused on...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Enterprise, specifically coding. And what you're seeing is that businesses are willing to pay for coding, code tokens, on a metered basis, let's call it like electricity. The more they use, the more they're willing to pay. And their usage is continues to scale and scale. Consumer is completely different. I mean, consumer is a thing that OpenAI prioritize. Consumers have a lower willingness to pay. Maybe only three or four percent of them are willing to convert to premium in the first place. And what they want is a $20 a month, all you can eat subscription. So the revenue simply doesn't scale the same way that enterprise does. And so if you want to tap into the scalable revenue source in the market right now, you have to go after enterprise. So, you know, again, where I would agree with the criticism of OpenAI is maybe they should have been more focused, but they need to be more focused specifically to pursue coding and enterprise. And if they don't catch up soon, to Travis's point, then you could see Anthropic take a lead here that, let's say, over the next one or two years could be insurmountable.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Just by the way, let me just say, say one thing is even though Anthropic's revenue has followed this graph, this exponential growth very predictably, it can't do that forever, right? full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I'll go first. Yeah, I mean look, I think it's pretty straightforward, which is that in the wake of the meeting in Islamabad, that the market is feeling confident and pricing in that the war is going to get resolved.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: The president also recently said that it's very close to being wrapped up, the military objectives are close to being achieved and he's made it sound like it's going to be resolved. Yes, a deal was not signed in Islamabad, I always thought that was an unrealistic expectation that these two countries, which are at war with each other and in fact have had hostile relations for almost 50 years, are going to resolve all their differences in 24 hours. It's not realistic.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: But the impression that the market I think has and clearly is trading on this is that that war is going to be what Donald Trump said, which is an excursion and something that is on its way to being resolved, that they've made progress. And J-Cal, you're right, the whole week has just been incredibly strong. I think by Tuesday, the market had recovered all of its losses since the start of the war. I think it made a new high yesterday on Wednesday and is making new highs, fresh highs today on Thursday. full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Just to qualify, I'm not speaking as a member of the administration.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Of course.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I'm not saying that I know something, okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You don't know anything. Do not aggregate this. No clipping.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: I'm not representing anyone and I don't know anything different than what any of you know. I'm just saying that I think this is what the market is clearly pricing in and I'm paying attention to what the statements are of the president and vice president.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And this Travis, you and I were talking the other day while you were slaughtering me in backgammon when you won our eight-point match, you're almost caught up. highlights: The proposed New York pied-à-terre tax could trigger a market crash by targeting the most elastic segment of buyers, contrasting with cities like Austin that successfully lower rents by increasing housing supply rather than imposing arbitrary taxes.

Anthropic's exponential revenue growth is currently outstripping OpenAI's by focusing heavily on enterprise coding, highlighting a shift where high-value business usage and metered "code tokens" are becoming more lucrative than flat-fee consumer subscriptions.

The AI industry is hitting a "physical wall" as data center construction faces increasing public opposition and power constraints, forcing frontier labs to transition from renting compute to owning their own energy and infrastructure to avoid service throttling.

While AI offers theoretical efficiency gains of 10x or more, the realization of the "autonomous enterprise" is being delayed by complex human change management and the current reality that AI agents still lack the "taste" or reasoning to operate without human oversight.
summary:
The podcast begins with a critique of a proposed New York pied-à-terre tax, which the hosts argue will stifle luxury real estate demand and drive investment toward more development-friendly cities like Austin. This transitions into a deep dive into the "identity crisis" at OpenAI as it faces aggressive competition from Anthropic. The panel analyzes Anthropic’s superior growth rate and rapid release cadence, debating whether OpenAI’s pivot toward enterprise and coding agents is a necessary focus or a sign of lost consumer momentum. A recurring theme is the "compute constraint," with the hosts arguing that owning physical infrastructure and securing energy is now the primary strategic hurdle for AI labs.

The discussion also explores the "peak bubble" behavior of companies like Allbirds pivoting to AI to salvage stock value, contrasted with a growing political backlash against data centers. The hosts highlight how NIMBYism, water usage concerns, and power grid limitations are creating a bottleneck for AI progress. They express concern over the public’s shifting sentiment toward AI, noting that the industry lacks effective spokespeople to articulate its benefits for healthcare and education. The group argues that unless AI labs solve their own power and land needs, they risk a "Friendster effect" where site performance and availability fail to keep up with user demand.

In the political segment, the hosts examine the resignation of Eric Swalwell, suggesting his exit was a coordinated move by Democratic "insiders" to clear the field for preferred candidates. This leads to a broader discussion on the financial success of congressional stock traders, like Nancy Pelosi and Ro Khanna, who benefit from exemptions to transparency rules that apply to the private sector. The episode concludes with a satirical game show called "The Price is Wrong," mocking overvalued "ZIRP-era" startups like Juicero and Quibi, and a market analysis suggesting that while valuation metrics are at all-time highs, the efficiency gains promised by AI might keep the "hardcore" tech companies resilient.
